title: practical english usage - Part 313
content:
One chicken curry and one fillet of sole. Enjoy!

in restaurants.

‘ Enjoy!' with no object is possible in informal English. It is often used by waiters ‘ We're going to Paris for the weekend.' * Enjoy yourselves!' I really enjoyed myself when I went to Rome.

To  talk about having a good time, we  can use enjoy myself/yourself, etc.

(     l-enfryedvery-Htuth;} ‘ Didyou enjoy the party? ‘ Yes, I enjoyed it very much.' Enjoy normally has an object.

Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section 31 vocabulary •  451  especial(ly) and special(ly) The song was specially written for his birthday.

These shoes were specially made for me.

Specially is used with a past participle to mean 'for a particular purpose’ .

4 specially         with    past    participles I drink a lot of coffee, especially when I'm working.

We go skiing quite a lot, especially in February.

We   prefer especially before prepositions and conjunctions.

3 especially           before     prepositions         and   conjunctions not he can. (    .. .-EspeeMfy-rnyfiitlwr-gtw*-...) All my family like music. My father, especially, goes to as many concerts as Especially follows a subject.

I like all kinds of fruit, especially apples.

The children are very noisy, especially when we have visitors.

We play a lot of tennis, especially on Sundays.

Especially is often used to mean 'above all’ .

2  especially           meaning       'above     all' It was not (e)specially cold.

Especially and specially can often both be used with the same meaning.

1  especially           and   specially 451  especial(ly)          and   special(ly) There isn't room for everybody to sit down.

Do you have time to look at this draft?

Time and room are often used to mean 'enough time’  and 'enough room’ .

I didn't quite have the money to pay for a meal.

1 hardly had the strength to take my clothes off.

The  article the can be used to mean 'enough*.

6 the    = enough;        leaving      out   enough For similar structures with too and too much/many, ►    610-611.

For other examples of for + object + infinitive, ►     113.

Those tomatoes aren't ripe enough for the children to eat (them).

The radio was small enough for me to put (it) in my pocket.

However, object pronouns are possible in structures with for.

Those tomatoes aren't ripe enough to eat. (     .. . to eat them.) not (     . . .-te-put-il-in-yeur-pcfeket;) The radio's small enough to put in your pocket.

after the infinitive in this case.

(For more about this structure, ►     101.4.) Object pronouns are not normally used The  subject of the sentence can be the object of the following infinitive.

5 It's  small      enough        to   put    in   your      pocket,        etc vocabulary •  452  even He left her ten years ago, but even now she still loves him.

Even now can mean 'in spite of everything that has happened’ .

He seems nice. Even so, I don't really trust him. (     .. .Even though,-1-don't Even so means ’ however’ , ’ in spite of that’ .

5 even      so;  even      now I'll do it if it kills me. (= ... even if it kills me.) We   sometimes use if to mean even if.

I wouldn't marry you even if you were the last man in the world.

(     Even although ...) Even though I didn't know anybody at the party, I had a nice time.

(    ... Even I become...) Even if I become a millionaire, 1 will always be a socialist.

if and though.

Even is not used alone as a conjunction, but we  can use even before 4 even      if  and   even      though not Everybody helped with the packing - even the dog. (     . .. also the dog.) Also (►  369) is not used to talk about surprising extremes.

3   even      and   also I haven't seen my family for months - not even my parents.

I work every day, even on Sundays.

Anybody can do this. Even a child can do it.

to emphasise.

and it can go just before other words and expressions that we  want Even goes at the beginning of a clause when it refers just to the subject; They're open every day. They're even open on Christmas Day.

He speaks lots of languages. He even speaks Esperanto.

(     Even she has broken ...) She has broken all her toys. She has even broken her bike.
